title: The SOG Encoding Pipeline and KISS-GS

learningObjectives.description: Trace the three SOG pipeline stages (quantization → 2D grid sorting → image codec compression) and identify the compression contribution of each stage for a representative scene
Explain why no 3DGS-specific codec is needed in the SOG pipeline when Gaussian attributes are arranged into a spatially sorted 2D grid and standard image codecs are applied
Describe how KISS-GS stacks GaussianSpa compaction, SOG encoding, and coding-aware finetuning to achieve 228× compression and identify the compression contribution of each component
Explain the SOG loss term for joint training and identify how a differentiable grid smoothness objective guides Gaussian arrangement during 3DGS optimization to improve final compression

content:
## Self-Organizing Gaussians (SOG)

SOG (Morgenstern et al., ECCV 2024) is the first method to fully integrate grid-based sorting into the 3DGS compression pipeline. It demonstrates that standard image codecs — designed for photographs — can compress 3DGS attribute grids by 20–40× without any 3DGS-specific codec.

## The Three-Stage Pipeline

### Stage 1: Quantization

Each attribute category is quantized to a reduced bit depth:

| Attribute | Bit depth | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| Position xyz | 16-bit (split: 8 high + 8 low per channel) | Two-pass encoding: high bytes form a smooth grid; low bytes are residuals |
| DC color | 8-bit | Perceptually, 256 levels is sufficient for base color |
| Scale \(s_1,s_2,s_3\) | 6-bit | 64 levels cover the observed scale distribution |
| Rotation \(q\) | 6-bit | 64 levels per quaternion component |
| Opacity \(\sigma_\alpha\) | 6-bit | 64 opacity levels |
| SH AC | 5-bit | 32 levels; high SH coefficients have small magnitude |

Total after quantization: much less than 59 × 32 bits. The non-linear parameter distortions (sigmoid for opacity, exp for scale) are applied in reverse before quantization to work in the linear parameter space.

### Stage 2: Sorting into 2D Grids

The \(N\) Gaussians are sorted onto a \(\sqrt{N} \times \sqrt{N}\) grid using FLAS or ShuffleSoftSort. The sorting key is a weighted combination of attributes, with position weighted most heavily.

After sorting, each of the 59 quantized attribute arrays becomes a 2D image. Attributes are grouped:
- **Position**: 6 images (x_high, x_low, y_high, y_low, z_high, z_low)
- **DC color**: 1 RGB image
- **Scale + rotation**: multi-layer images
- **SH AC**: 15-layer multi-channel image (45 coefficients × 1/3 channels per layer)

### Stage 3: Image Compression

Each attribute image is compressed with a standard image codec:
- **JPEG XL** (lossless mode for high-precision attributes, lossy for SH)
- **WebP** (good lossy compression with adjustable quality)

Because the sorted grids are spatially smooth (the sorting step maximized local correlation), the DCT/transform in the image codec finds smooth patches with concentrated low-frequency energy.

## Results

For the Truck scene (originally 639.7 MB at float32):
- After quantization only: ~107 MB
- After quantization + sorting + JPEG XL: **28.8 MB** (≈22× overall compression)

Key advantages of SOG:
- **No structural modifications**: works on any existing 3DGS output, no retraining needed (though optional finetuning helps)
- **Hardware decodable**: the decoder just decompresses standard image files and dequantizes — no custom CUDA kernel needed
- **Extensible**: any improvement in 3DGS training that produces smoother Gaussian distributions will automatically improve SOG compression

## Adaptation (Finetuning)

After the encode/decode cycle, a small quantization error is introduced. Optional finetuning minimizes this:

\[\mathcal{L}_{finetune} = \mathcal{L}_{render} + \lambda_{smooth}\mathcal{L}_{smooth}\]

where \(\mathcal{L}_{smooth}\) penalizes variation between neighboring Gaussians in the sorted grid (further reducing the residuals that the image codec must encode). This adaptation step yields ~2× additional reduction without further quality loss.

## KISS-GS: Combining All Three Approaches

KISS-GS (Keep It Simple and Scalable GS) demonstrates the combined power of all three compression strategies:

| Component | Method | Compression |
|---|---|---|
| Compaction | GaussianSpa + GaussianPop | 15.7× |
| Encoding | HD sorting + SH via codebooks | 6.6× |
| Adaptation | Coding-aware finetuning | 2.2× |
| **Total** | | **~228×** |

Starting from a 1.3 GB representation: KISS-GS produces approximately 6 MB while maintaining competitive visual quality. This demonstrates that compaction, encoding, and adaptation compound multiplicatively — each stage builds on the previous one.

## The SOG Loss for Joint Training

For optimal results, SOG proposes adding a smoothness loss during 3DGS training:

\[\mathcal{L}_{SOG} = \sum_{(i,j) \in \text{neighbors}} \|A_{grid(i)} - A_{grid(j)}\|^2\]

This encourages neighboring grid cells to have similar attributes — exactly the property that image codecs exploit. The sort order is updated periodically during training, so the grid structure is jointly optimized with the Gaussian attributes.

terms.name: Total finetuning objective
terms.desc: The loss minimized during the optional post-encode adaptation step. Gradients update only the Gaussian attributes — the grid structure and codec are fixed. The goal is to recover quality lost during quantization and image compression.

terms.name: Photometric rendering loss
terms.desc: Standard L1 + D-SSIM loss between rendered and ground-truth views. Ensures the finetuned Gaussians still reproduce the training photographs accurately.

terms.name: Smoothness loss weight
terms.desc: Controls how strongly the finetuning pulls attributes toward grid-smooth values. Higher values produce more compressible grids but may sacrifice rendering quality at high compression ratios.

terms.name: Grid smoothness penalty
terms.desc: Penalizes large differences between neighboring cells in the sorted 2D grid. Reducing this term means smaller residuals for the image codec to encode — directly improving the compression ratio of the subsequent JPEG XL / WebP pass.

terms.name: Sum over adjacent grid cell pairs
terms.desc: Iterates over every pair of cells that are immediate neighbors (N/E/S/W) in the 2D sorted grid. Only adjacent pairs contribute — this is a local loss, not a global one.

terms.name: Attribute vector at grid position i
terms.desc: The full 59-dimensional Gaussian attribute vector assigned to grid cell i after sorting. grid(i) maps the grid coordinate to the Gaussian index.

terms.name: Squared attribute distance between neighbors
terms.desc: How dissimilar two adjacent grid cells are in attribute space. A smooth grid has small values here everywhere. Minimizing this sum is exactly what good sorting achieves — and what image codecs exploit via local prediction.

references.label: Morgenstern et al. 2024
references.title: Compact 3D Scene Representation via Self-Organizing Gaussian Grids

references.label: Wen et al. 2024
references.title: GaussianSpa: An Optimizing-Sparsifying Simplification Framework for Compact and High-Quality 3D Gaussian Splatting
